Two More Breaches. Two More Lessons About Third-Party Risk.  Hims & Hers, the telehealth company, disclosed that a February cyberattack compromised a customer-support system, exposing personal information across multiple states.  Mister Guns, a Texas-based gun retailer, is notifying 21,225 people that a November hack exposed names, Social Security numbers, concealed handgun licenses, and other sensitive personal data.  These incidents share a common thread: attackers targeted supporting systems, not just core databases, to get what they needed.   Customer-support platforms, CRM tools, and third-party integrations are frequently the weakest link in an organization's security posture. Adversaries know this. Most detection tools are tuned for the core network, not the edges. 중국 해커, FBI 감시 네트워크 침투. ‘중대 사이버 사건’으로 규정   - 미국 국가안보에 심각한 위협… 중국의 지속적 사이버 공격 재확인  폴리티코는 1일 FBI가 최근 중국 연계 해커의 내부 감시 시스템 침투를 ‘중대 사이버 사건’으로 공식 선언했다고 보도했다. 이는 미국 국가안보에 중대한 위험을 초래하는 것으로 판단된 사례다.  FBI는 지난 3월 4일 의회에 해당 시스템에 대한 의심스러운 활동을 보고했다. 해당 시스템은 법 집행 과정에서 수집된 민감 정보, 즉 범죄 용의자 등의 통화 기록과 인터넷 활동 데이터를 담고 있다. 침투는 상업 인터넷 서비스 제공업체의 공급자 인프라를 이용한 정교한 방식으로 이뤄졌다.  월스트리트저널은 3월 6일 중국 정부와 연계된 해커가 FBI의 비분류 시스템에 침입했다고 처음 보도했다. 이 시스템에는 법원 명령에 따른 감시 자료(펜 레지스터·트랩 앤 트레이스)가 저장돼 있으며, 통화 발·착신 기록, IP 주소, 웹사이트 방문 기록 등이 포함된다. 다만 통화 내용이나 디지털 메시지 본문은 들어 있지 않다.  FBI 대변인은 “FBI 네트워크에서 의심스러운 활동을 식별하고 대응했다”며 기술적 조치를 취했다고 밝혔다. 그러나 침투 규모와 피해 정도는 아직 공개되지 않았다. 전문가들은 중국 해커가 미국 법 집행 감시 시스템을 반복적으로 노린 사례 중 최신 사례라고 지적했다. politico.com/news/2026/04/01…

【독점 분석】기관 Benchmark, Palo Alto Networks 첫 분석 보고서 ‘매수’ 의견 제시… 목표주가 200달러  “플랫폼 리더, 2030회계연도 차세대 보안(NGS) 연간 반복 매출(ARR) 200억 달러 향해 순항 중”  Benchmark는 Palo Alto Networks에 대해 ‘매수’ 의견을 제시하며, 이 회사를 사이버 보안 섹터 내 핵심 보유 종목(Core Holding)으로 평가했다. 보고서는 이 회사의 차세대 보안(NGS) 연간 반복 매출(ARR) 성장 전망에 대해 “비교적 명확한 가시성”을 확보했다고 분석했다. NGS ARR은 2026회계연도 85억 7천만 달러에서 2030회계연도에는 200억 달러로 두 배 이상 성장할 전망이며, 연평균 성장률(CAGR)은 32.4%에 달해 고성장 동종 업계 기업들과 비교해도 뛰어난 수치라는 평가다.  Benchmark는 애플리케이션 소프트웨어와 달리 사이버 보안 분야는 “AI/생성형 AI의 혁신적인 변화에 대해 충분한 경쟁적 해자(Competitive Moat)를 보유하고 있다”고 강조했다. 또한 AI 슈퍼 사이클을 과거 클라우드 컴퓨팅 및 디지털 전환(DX) 물결과 유사한 장기적 호재(Long-term Tailwind)로 판단했다.  Palo Alto Networks는 2025회계연도 말 기준 ‘52.8 법칙(성장률+마진율 합산 지표)’을 달성했으며, 2026회계연도에는 60에 근접할 것으로 전망된다. 보고서는 AI 및 양자 보안(Quantum Security)에서 발생할 수 있는 잠재적 추가 수익은 현재 전망치에 포함되지 않았다고 덧붙였다.  목표주가 200달러는 2027회계연도 예상 기업가치 대비 매출(EV/Revenue) 배수 12.3배를 적용한 것이다. 동종 업계 평균 배수는 7.9배 수준이다.  우리분석에 따르면, Palo Alto Networks의 CEO가 3월 27일 주당 146.87달러에 약 1000만 달러 규모의 자사주를 매수한 것으로 확인됐다.  $crwd $s $ftnt #PaloAltoNetworks #PANW #사이버보안 #NGS #ARR #AI보안 #Benchmark #목표주가 #美증시 #투자전략 #사이버보안주 #정보보안

Supply chain attack on Axios npm package exposed 100 million weekly downloads to platform-specific RATs. Malicious versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4 deployed March 31, 2026 for 3-hour window before detection.  Technical breakdown: • Fake dependency "plain-crypto-js" executes post-install, contacts 142[.]11[.]206[.]73 for OS-specific payload delivery • Windows: Downloads PS1, copies powershell.exe to %PROGRAMDATA%\wt.exe, executes with bypass flags • MacOS: Deploys "com.apple.act.mond" binary via zsh execution • Linux: Python-based RAT with remote access capabilities • Attack vector: Supply chain compromise (T1195.002) with automatic payload deployment  DFIR teams should immediately: - Hunt for plain-crypto-js dependency in package.json/node_modules - Monitor for network connections to 142[.]11[.]206[.]73 and sfrclak[.]com - Check %PROGRAMDATA%\wt.exe on Windows systems - Scan for the provided SHA256 hashes across endpoints - Rotate all credentials on affected systems  Organizations using Axios should rollback to v1.14.0/v0.30.3 and assume credential compromise if malicious versions were downloaded. Full IOC list available in Cisco Talos report.  #DFIR_Radar

The Iran-Israel-US conflict just gave us the world's first AI-native information war. And we weren't ready.  Our latest report at FalconFeeds tracked what happened after Operation Epic Fury launched on February 28, 2026. Within hours, social media was flooded with AI-generated deepfakes, manipulated satellite imagery, and recycled footage -  all designed to shape perception before the truth could catch up.  The scale is staggering:  → 110+ unique AI deepfakes pushing pro-Iran narratives identified in just two weeks (NYT) → Hundreds of millions of views on fabricated content (BBC Verify) → A single individual in Pakistan ran 31 hacked X accounts posting AI-generated war footage - motivated not by ideology, but by platform revenue sharing → Google's own AI search validated a fake "CIA outpost destroyed in Dubai" claim using footage from a 2015 residential fire → Netanyahu was forced to release proof-of-life videos after deepfake speculation spiraled globally  What's new isn't wartime propaganda -  it's three compounding factors:  1. Technical barriers to hyper-realistic fabrication have effectively disappeared 2. Platform economics financially reward viral misinformation at the individual creator level 3. AI verification tools didn't just fail to help - they became amplification vectors  Russia and China amplified Iranian AI content without needing coordination. A shared anti-Western interest created organic alignment. Video game footage was passed off as combat. State media used AI-altered satellite imagery to fabricate strike successes.  The cognitive battlespace is no longer secondary. It's where legitimacy, morale, and public consent are won and lost.  The full open-source intelligence report covers documented cases, actor mapping, narrative propagation timelines, platform failures, and geopolitical consequences. I'm not dissing @IntCyberDigest. What he wrote appears to be factually accurate. I don't think he is being misleading. My dumb little opinion piece was written to be more educational toward non-cybersecurity people because I think it's important to emphasize what this sort of compromise means and what the Threat Actors are capable (or incapable) of doing.

Yeah, so pretty much @IntCyberDigest got wind of Adobe allegedly* being compromised by a Threat Actor operating under the moniker Mr. Racoon, by all accounts it appears this compromise is legitimate. However, I believe there are some caveats that need to be emphasized to avoid confusion.  Adobe's internal networks were not compromised. Adobe had their helpdesk system compromised. Yes, both are Adobe, but a compromise to helpdesk support is different than a network compromise. Adobe, like many other large companies, intentionally segregates different business components.  A network compromise could potentially impact the organization itself, proprietary source code, etc. A helpdesk compromise will likely unveil sensitive information on users.  In simpler terms, anyone who submitted a helpdesk ticket to Adobe, or requested assistance in any capacity, could be impacted.  However, the scope of impact is unknown at this time. It is being reported that 13,000,000 support tickets have been exfiltrated, but that could be anything from a simple question to something more consequential like billing questions. Furthermore, we don't know how far these support tickets to back. But 13 million is an astronomically large number.  A closing thought on this is that this compromise was the result of a BPO employee (outsourced labor). This BPO employee (according to the Threat Actor) was infected with malware, they WERE NOT an Insider Threat. Once the BPO employee was compromised the Threat Actors pivoted by phishing a manager.  More information is needed, Adobe will likely make a statement later (or not depending on what their legal team suggests). It has been a very dramatic week in cybersecurity

Or no one will and I'll do it myself later on, whatever.  Either way, you can do some pretty silly shenanigans with the Windows registry but it requires some tom foolery.  C:\Windows\System32\config\SYSTEM is the actual file (and full file image path) for the Windows registry. The registry is loaded into memory when your OS boots (some fancy Windows internals stuff, whatever).  This file is locked and requires administrative privileges (and a few other things to access it). Even then, it cannot be modified. However, you can do two mildly interesting things:  1. Load it into memory using NtLoadKey. Any changes made will not be present until the OS is restarted (over simplifying, some edge cases exist).  2. Read it into memory with NtOpenFile and NtReadFile (read-only).  This can be a little tricky, depending on where your payload is executing, but you can abuse some Windows components to access these files without a UAC prompt.  ICMLuaUtil!AllowAccessToTheWorld is an undocumented method. I have discussed it in the past (no one else has, no idea why), and using this method you can make any directory "global" to all users on the machine. My hypothesis is that you can abuse ICMLuaUtil!AllowAccessToTheWorld to change the ACL of C:\Windows\System32\config\ to allow your payload to access SYSTEM (offline registry) without triggering UAC.  For case one, using NtLoadKey and using an offline registry hive for registry modification and AV/EDR evasion isn't a new concept. But basically, use ICMLuaUtil!AllowAccessToTheWorld to access SYSTEM offline registry hive, make any modifications using RegSetValue, then "flush" the hive back to disk with NtUnloadKey and restart the machine.  For case two, use ICMLuaUtil!AllowAccessToTheWorld to access SYSTEM and read the file into memory with NtOpenFile and NtReadFile. Once loaded into memory, manually parse the registry (REGF parsing) to query registry keys without using any Windows registry API calls. However, this would be limited to read-only procedures. Case two would be very sneaky and would bamboozle security products.
